body_txt: In many cultures, polka dots are associated with positivity. In Japan, they are associated with the sun, the moon, and infinite energy. In Filipino culture, they represent prosperity (due to their coin shapes), and many Filipinos wear polka-dotted clothes to celebrate the new year. Ditto for Chinese Feng Shui culture. In the 1950s, thanks to the likes of Marilyn Monroe and Lucille Ball, they became associated with the Spoiled Sweet of the United States of America. These little dots carry a universal appeal, so it may actually shock you to find out that at one point in Europe, they were stigmatized because of their resemblance to measles and sickness. Nowadays, they're celebrated by fashion and art enthusiasts all over the globe. In fiction, creators may try to convey energetic and cheerful characters with polka dots. This could be because they look silly (polka dots are a common choice for Goofy Print Underwear , after all) and stylish at the same time. Characters who wear these kinds of clothes may often be The Pollyanna or Cheerful Child . Being a fashion trope, this is usually more common in female characters. This trope often overlaps with Iconic Outfit (for example, Minnie Mouse and Dusty Rhodes ). It can also overlap with I Was Quite a Fashion Victim , Cloud Cuckoolander , Red Is Heroic note Red is a common choice for polka dots , and Pink Is Feminine .
